summary: In King’s Abbot, Mrs. Ferrars dies from an overdose of veronal on the night of 16-17 September after having previously murdered her husband Ashley Ferrars and endured blackmail. Roger Ackroyd, who intended to marry her, tells Dr. James Sheppard that Mrs. Ferrars confessed the murder and said someone was blackmailing her, possibly someone connected with his household. A posthumous letter from Mrs. Ferrars reaches Ackroyd during Sheppard’s visit and begins to name the blackmailer. Sheppard, who is the blackmailer, murders Ackroyd before leaving the study, using a Tunisian dagger taken earlier from the silver table. He leaves the study window apparently relevant to the crime, plants Ralph Paton’s shoe-prints to implicate Ralph, removes or later retrieves key evidence, and uses Ackroyd’s dictaphone with a timed mechanism so that Ackroyd’s recorded voice is heard at about 9:30 p.m., creating the illusion that Ackroyd is alive after Sheppard has gone. Sheppard also places a false telephone call summoning himself back to Fernly, giving himself the opportunity to be present at discovery and remove the dictaphone. Suspicion falls heavily on Ralph Paton because he is missing, has financial motives, has quarreled with Ackroyd, is secretly married to Ursula Bourne, and his shoe-prints appear at the study window. Other suspects have motives or secrets: Flora Ackroyd stole money and lied about seeing her uncle alive; Parker listened at doors and has a dubious past; Mrs. Ackroyd searched for financial papers; Miss Russell concealed her relationship to Charles Kent; Charles Kent secretly visited Fernly; Raymond was in debt; and Blunt concealed his feelings for Flora. Hercule Poirot reconstructs the true sequence by detecting the time discrepancy in Sheppard’s walk to the gate, the missing dictaphone, the staged footprints, the missing dagger opportunity, the black bag capable of carrying the dictaphone, and Sheppard’s access, motive, mechanical knowledge, and false evidence. Poirot confronts Sheppard privately and allows him the alternative of suicide to spare Caroline public disgrace. Sheppard writes a final confession, admits that he blackmailed Mrs. Ferrars and murdered Ackroyd to prevent exposure, and prepares to kill himself with veronal.

witnesses.name: Dr. James Sheppard
witnesses.statement: Narrator, village doctor, first medical observer of Mrs. Ferrars’s death, dinner guest at Fernly, apparent discoverer of Ackroyd’s murder, recorder of interviews, and final confessor. He reports Mrs. Ferrars’s veronal death, Ackroyd’s disclosures about Mrs. Ferrars’s confession and blackmail, the arrival of her letter, his own departure at 8:50 p.m., the stranger near the gates, the telephone call summoning him back, the crime scene, and the investigation. His narration omits and falsifies decisive facts: he had blackmailed Mrs. Ferrars, murdered Ackroyd before leaving the study, used the dictaphone to fake Ackroyd’s later voice, staged Ralph’s footprints, made the false telephone call, and retrieved incriminating evidence. His final apologia corrects the deception and confesses the crime.

witnesses.name: Hercule Poirot
witnesses.statement: Retired detective living next door under the locally distorted name Porrott. He gathers witness statements, tests alibis, protects Ralph Paton from premature arrest, identifies concealed relationships and lies, and reconstructs the murder. He concludes that Ackroyd was already dead when his voice was heard at 9:30 p.m.; that the voice came from a dictaphone; that the murderer needed knowledge of the dictaphone, mechanical aptitude, access to Ralph’s shoes, access to the dagger, a receptacle such as a black bag, and opportunity to remove the machine at discovery; and that these conditions identify Dr. Sheppard. His deductions resolve the case.

witnesses.name: Roger Ackroyd
witnesses.statement: Victim and key pre-murder informant. He tells Sheppard that Mrs. Ferrars confessed to poisoning Ashley Ferrars, that she had been blackmailed, that she refused to name the blackmailer, and that he expected a message from her. He receives her letter after dinner and begins reading it aloud before deciding to read the rest alone. His information supplies the motive for his own murder: the letter would expose Sheppard as blackmailer.

witnesses.name: Mrs. Ferrars
witnesses.statement: Dead before the main murder but a central evidentiary source through her confession to Ackroyd and posthumous letter. She admits murdering her husband Ashley Ferrars, explains that she was blackmailed, and writes to Ackroyd intending to identify the blackmailer. The letter is intercepted after Ackroyd’s death, preventing immediate revelation, but its existence explains Sheppard’s motive.

witnesses.name: Caroline Sheppard
witnesses.statement: Dr. Sheppard’s sister and a major village intelligence-gatherer. She reports early gossip about Mrs. Ferrars’s death, correctly suspects Mrs. Ferrars killed her husband and committed suicide from remorse, learns Ralph Paton is staying at the Three Boars, reports seeing or hearing Ralph with a woman in the woods and overhearing his anxiety about Ackroyd’s will, tracks local gossip, and later helps Poirot by inquiring into Ralph’s footwear, establishing that Ralph had boots and that the incriminating shoe-prints could have been staged with another pair of his shoes.

witnesses.name: Parker
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s butler. He admits bringing in the evening post, including Mrs. Ferrars’s letter, and is suspected of listening at the study door. He reports the state of the study after the body is found, including the pulled-out chair, which helps Poirot infer that something on the table had to be removed after discovery. He also denies having telephoned Sheppard, undermining Sheppard’s account of the summons. His own secretiveness and past make him suspect, but his material crime-scene observations are useful and mostly confirmed.

witnesses.name: Geoffrey Raymond
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s secretary. He provides household timing evidence, says he heard Ackroyd’s voice from the study around 9:30 p.m. saying he did not want to be disturbed, and supplies information about Ackroyd’s business affairs and dictation habits. He later admits private financial embarrassment, which creates a possible motive but does not implicate him in the murder. His hearing of the voice is accurate as an auditory observation but misinterpreted because it was the dictaphone, not the living Ackroyd.

witnesses.name: Flora Ackroyd
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s niece and Ralph’s supposed fiancee. She initially claims to have seen Ackroyd alive after dinner, strengthening the false later time of death and protecting herself. She later retracts this and confesses that she went to the study to steal money from Ackroyd’s room and did not actually see him alive. She also provides important evidence that the Tunisian dagger was missing from the silver table when she examined it, narrowing the opportunity to someone who had taken it earlier.

witnesses.name: Ralph Paton
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s adopted son and principal false suspect. He is secretly married to Ursula Bourne, is financially dependent on Ackroyd, had quarreled with him, and disappears after the murder, making himself look guilty. When found by Poirot, he states that he wore boots on the fatal night, not the shoes whose prints were found at the study window. This helps prove the footprints were planted. His concealment and flight damage his reliability, but his core exculpatory footwear information is confirmed.

witnesses.name: Ursula Bourne
witnesses.statement: Fernly parlourmaid, later revealed as Ralph Paton’s secret wife. She explains her concealed marriage to Ralph, her dismissal after Ackroyd learned of it, her meetings with Ralph, and the personal conflict that made Ralph afraid of disinheritance. Her story resolves the mystery of Ralph’s secret woman and supplies an innocent explanation for several suspicious meetings and Ralph’s disappearance.

witnesses.name: Miss Russell
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s housekeeper. She asks Sheppard about drug addiction, rare poisons, and curare, creating suspicion. She later explains that her concern relates to Charles Kent, her illegitimate son, whose drug habit and visit to Fernly she wished to conceal. Her concealment misdirects suspicion, but her later account helps identify Kent’s role as a red herring rather than the murderer.

witnesses.name: Charles Kent
witnesses.statement: A rough young stranger seen by Sheppard near the Fernly gates after Sheppard leaves. He is later identified as Miss Russell’s illegitimate son and a drug user. He admits visiting Fernly secretly to see his mother and gives an account of his movements. His presence explains the stranger at the gate and initially appears incriminating, but timing and corroboration show he did not murder Ackroyd.

witnesses.name: Major Hector Blunt
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s friend and houseguest. He supplies observations from the household, participates in the investigation, confirms or contests timing and behaviour, and is emotionally connected to Flora Ackroyd. His guarded manner creates suspicion, but his material evidence is mainly observational and not central to the murder mechanism.

witnesses.name: Mrs. Cecil Ackroyd
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s widowed sister-in-law. She provides information about household financial pressures, Flora’s prospects, settlements, and her own dependence on Ackroyd. Her evasions and interest in money make her suspicious, and she conceals self-interested conduct around Ackroyd’s papers, but she ultimately contributes motive context rather than evidence of the murder mechanism.

witnesses.name: Inspector Raglan
witnesses.statement: Police investigator. He reports and interprets official evidence: the body, weapon, study door and window, footprints, timing, suspects, and searches. He treats Ralph Paton as the likely murderer for much of the case because of motive, disappearance, and shoe evidence. His factual collection is useful, but his central theory is wrong until Poirot’s reconstruction corrects it.

witnesses.name: Colonel Melrose
witnesses.statement: Chief constable or senior local police authority. He helps frame the official inquiry, coordinates with Poirot and Raglan, and receives reports about the murder scene, suspects, and household evidence. His contributions are mainly administrative and investigative rather than eyewitness testimony.

witnesses.name: Mr. Hammond
witnesses.statement: Ackroyd’s solicitor. He provides the terms of Ackroyd’s will and financial arrangements, clarifying who benefits from Ackroyd’s death and how Ralph, Flora, Mrs. Ackroyd, and others stand financially. This shapes motive analysis and confirms that several suspects had financial interests, though not the true motive.

witnesses.name: Miss Ganett
witnesses.statement: Village gossip and observer. She circulates reports about Mrs. Ferrars, Ackroyd, Flora, Ralph, blackmail, and local movements. Some information reflects genuine local observation, but much is speculative, inferential, or socially biased. Her evidence contributes atmosphere and suspicion more than decisive proof.

witnesses.name: Colonel Carter
witnesses.statement: Village gossip source during the mah-jong evening. He reports hearing that blackmail is involved and speculates that a woman is behind the affair. His information reflects rumor rather than firsthand evidence, though the existence of blackmail is ultimately true.

witnesses.name: Annie
witnesses.statement: The Sheppards’ parlourmaid. She passes early household news to Caroline about Mrs. Ferrars’s death and serves as part of Caroline’s informal intelligence network. Her contribution is minor and hearsay-based.

witnesses.name: The milkman
witnesses.statement: Unnamed local tradesman who tells Caroline about Mrs. Ferrars’s death, having heard it from the Ferrars household cook. He contributes early reporting but only through hearsay.

witnesses.name: Mrs. Ferrars’s cook
witnesses.statement: Unnamed domestic servant at King’s Paddock. She is the apparent source of early village information that Mrs. Ferrars has died. Her contribution is limited to reporting the death before formal inquiry details are known.

witnesses.name: The barmaid at the Dog and Whistle
witnesses.statement: Provides corroboration about Charles Kent’s movements, appearance, and poor boots, helping separate Kent from the staged Ralph Paton shoe-prints and weakening the theory that Kent made the incriminating marks at Ackroyd’s window.

witnesses.name: The representative of the dictaphone company
witnesses.statement: Provides the crucial fact that Ackroyd had obtained or been supplied with a dictaphone. This allows Poirot to infer that the 9:30 p.m. voice was a recording, not proof Ackroyd was alive, and that the missing object removed from the study was the dictaphone.

witnesses.name: Mrs. Folliott
witnesses.statement: Provides background relevant to Miss Russell and Charles Kent, helping Poirot uncover Kent’s identity and relationship to Miss Russell. Her information supports the explanation of Miss Russell’s suspicious behaviour and Kent’s visit as a separate concealed family matter rather than the murder plot.
